does: This paper proves a fundamental theoretical impossibility result for machine unlearning in multi-stage training pipelines. The authors show that local unlearning methods, which operate only on gradients computed from data to be forgotten, cannot achieve Retrain Equivalence—behavior identical to retraining from scratch—when models undergo sequential training stages with different data distributions and objectives. They establish this impossibility through path-dependence theory, demonstrating that a model's behavior during unlearning depends on the specific order of training stages encountered during learning. The work validates this theoretical finding empirically across large language models including Llama and Qwen (1B to 14B parameters) undergoing multi-stage post-training and alignment procedures.
connects: This work extends the machine unlearning literature, which has primarily focused on single-stage training scenarios with i.i.d. data. Prior research formalized Retrain Equivalence as the gold-standard unlearning target and developed local gradient-based methods to approximate it. The paper responds to the gap between idealized single-stage assumptions and modern machine learning pipelines, particularly large language model training where fine-tuning, alignment, and instruction-tuning stages are standard. It builds on foundational work establishing that unlearning faces fundamental algorithmic limits, while shifting the frame from computational complexity to structural impossibility imposed by training dynamics. The path-dependence analysis connects to broader research on how training history and optimization trajectories shape model behavior.
enables: This impossibility result motivates fundamental rethinking of unlearning objectives and methods for practical multi-stage training. It suggests that researchers must either develop weaker unlearning guarantees tailored to multi-stage settings, explore non-local unlearning approaches that modify model architecture or training procedures, or accept approximations to Retrain Equivalence. The work opens questions about whether alternative equivalence notions can be achieved when training stages are reordered, or whether metadata about training history must be incorporated into unlearning algorithms. For practitioners, it indicates that standard local unlearning guarantees may not apply to modern LLM pipelines, highlighting the need for stage-aware unlearning methods and revised evaluation criteria for forgetting quality in sequential training contexts.
